stem: Consider the homogeneous system \( A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0} \), where \[ A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & [[s]] & [[t]] & [[m14]] \\ [[p]] & [[m22]] & [[m23]] & [[m24]] \\ [[q]] & [[m32]] & [[m33]] & [[m34]] \end{bmatrix}. \] What is the dimension of its solution space (the null space of \( A \))?
solution: The matrix has \( 4 \) columns and rank \( [[rankval]] \). By the rank–nullity theorem the solution space has dimension \( 4 - [[rankval]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the rank of \( A \), not the nullity; they are swapped.
This overcounts the free variables by one.
This undercounts the free variables by one.
This is the number of columns, which counts every variable as free.
This assumes the only solution is the trivial one.
This guesses a fixed value without applying rank–nullity.
This overcounts the free variables.
This guesses a fixed value without applying rank–nullity.

stem: A linear transformation \( T : \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \to \mathbb{R}^{[[m]]} \) has rank \( [[r]] \). Since the domain of \( T \) is the solution space's ambient space, what is the dimension of the solution space of \( T(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0} \) (that is, \( \dim(\ker T) \))?
solution: By the rank–nullity theorem, \( \dim(\ker T) + \operatorname{rank}(T) = \dim(\text{domain}) \), so \( \dim(\ker T) = [[n]] - [[r]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the rank itself, not the nullity.
This subtracts the rank from the codomain dimension instead of the domain dimension.
This adds instead of subtracting.
This is the domain dimension, as if the map were entirely non-injective.
This is the codomain dimension, unrelated to the kernel.
This overcounts the nullity by one.
This undercounts the nullity by one.
This adds the codomain dimension and the rank.

stem: Consider the homogeneous system of equations in the four unknowns \( x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4 \): \[ [[a11]]x_1 + [[a12]]x_2 + [[b1]]x_3 + [[c1]]x_4 = 0, \] \[ [[a21]]x_1 + [[a22]]x_2 + [[b2]]x_3 + [[c2]]x_4 = 0. \] What is the dimension of the solution space?
solution: The coefficient matrix has two rows, and the \( x_1, x_2 \) columns form an invertible \( 2\times 2 \) block (since \( [[a11]]\cdot[[a22]] - [[a12]]\cdot[[a21]] \ne 0 \)), so the matrix has rank \( 2 \). By rank–nullity the solution space has dimension \( 4 - 2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the rank (number of independent equations), not the nullity.
This is the total number of unknowns, as if every equation were redundant.
This assumes the system has only the trivial solution.
This undercounts the free variables by one.
This overcounts the free variables by one.
This overcounts the rank by only one equation's worth.
This adds instead of subtracting the rank.

stem: The matrix \( A \) below is already in row echelon form: \[ A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] & [[d]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & [[row3d]] \end{bmatrix}. \] Without further row reduction, what is the dimension of the null space of \( A \) (the solution space of \( A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0} \))?
solution: \( A \) is already in echelon form with \( [[rankval]] \) nonzero (pivot) rows, so \( \operatorname{rank}(A) = [[rankval]] \). By rank–nullity, the null space has dimension \( 4 - [[rankval]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the rank (number of pivot rows), not the nullity.
This is the number of columns, as if every column were free.
This assumes every column is a pivot column.
This overcounts the free columns by one.
This undercounts the free columns by one.
This miscounts the pivot rows.
This miscounts the pivot rows.
